topic: Darwin hospital trip — kidney check-up results stable

turns.content: Just got back from Darwin yesterday. Long drive. I am still tired from it.
Long drives can really take it out of you. What took you to Darwin?

Had a check-up. Routine thing, results were fine. But it meant two nights away and the whole drive there and back. I took the kids because my cousin was looking after them in Palmerston while I was at the appointment.
Glad to hear the results were fine. Two nights away plus the driving is a lot of effort for a routine appointment. Is Darwin the closest place for those kinds of appointments?

For the specialist, yes. There is a clinic closer but for this one I had to go to Royal Darwin. Early morning appointment Wednesday so I drove down Tuesday afternoon. Stayed with my cousin in Palmerston both nights.
Having family to stay with in Palmerston makes it more manageable at least. Did you get to do anything else while you were there, or was it all just the appointment and the drive?

I took the kids to the waterfront on Wednesday afternoon since we were already there. Bunjil has been asking about the wave pool for months. He is 10 and his mates at school told him about it. So I thought, we are here, might as well.
That's a nice way to make the trip worthwhile for the kids. Did Bunjil enjoy it?

Would not get out of the water. I had to bribe him with an ice cream in the end. Tali was too cool for the wave pool. She sat on the edge reading a book the whole time. She did look up once when a big wave knocked some tourist kid over. Almost smiled.
Sounds about right for a teenager. At least Bunjil got his wish. Was it your first time at the waterfront?

No, I have been there before. Years ago. It has changed a lot. More restaurants and shops. More people. Darwin keeps growing and every time I go back there is something new built somewhere. I do not know how people live with all that noise and movement all the time.
Cities do have a way of creeping outward. The contrast must be pretty sharp coming from a quieter area. How long is the drive back for you?

About three hours depending on the road. Some sections are rough at the moment. End of dry season and the graders have not been through. But I do not mind the drive back. Once you get past Adelaide River and the country opens up, you can feel the quiet come back. I had the windows down for the last hour. Hot but the air smells right. Eucalypt and red dirt.
There's something restorative about that transition from city to open country. The smell of eucalypt and red dirt is hard to replicate. Were the kids alright for the drive?

Bunjil fell asleep before we got to the highway. Tali had her headphones on. It was quiet in a different way. Good quiet. I had time to think. That is the thing about the drive, it gives you space. In Darwin everything is close together and fast. Out here the distances make you slow down.

That kind of thinking time is valuable. Some people find long drives draining but it sounds like for you it's the opposite -- the drive home is where you decompress. Did you get everything sorted that you needed to in Darwin?

Yes. Appointment done, results fine, kids got an outing. Now I am back and I have three days of work to catch up on. My patrol sector does not look after itself. But it is good to be home. I slept proper well last night for the first time in three days. My cousin's house is on a busy street and I could hear traffic all night.
Traffic noise when you're used to quiet is hard to sleep through. Glad you're back and rested. Good luck catching up on the backlog.
Thanks. Back to normal now. The country was waiting for me.
Sounds like a good place to come home to. Take it easy on the catch-up.
